summary: This content explains what an escrow account is and why it may be used during a mortgage transaction. It provides education-only guidance and directs any questions about pricing, rates, or personal qualification to a licensed Loan Officer.

key_steps.title: Learn what escrow means
key_steps.instruction: An escrow account is commonly used to collect and pay certain ongoing costs, such as property taxes and insurance, depending on lender and loan requirements.

key_steps.title: Understand what may be included
key_steps.instruction: Review which items are commonly associated with escrow (for example, taxes and insurance) and confirm the specific items for your situation using your lender’s documents.

key_steps.title: Review your disclosures carefully
key_steps.instruction: When you receive lender-provided disclosures, check how escrow is described and ask questions if anything is unclear.

key_steps.title: Ask about changes over time
key_steps.instruction: Escrow amounts can change when taxes or insurance change. If you see a change, ask how it was calculated and what changed.

faq_items.question: Is an escrow account always required?
faq_items.answer: Whether escrow is required can vary by lender, loan program, and other factors. Your Loan Officer can confirm what applies to your specific loan and documentation.

faq_items.question: Why might my escrow payment change?
faq_items.answer: Escrow amounts can change if the underlying costs change, such as property taxes or insurance premiums. Your lender may provide an analysis explaining the change.

escalation_triggers.trigger_text: Which loan is best for me given my situation?
What will my monthly payment be with escrow?
